title: Bandanawaz Gisudaraz
description: Khwaja Sayyid Muhammad Husayni Gesudaraz Bandanawaz (1321–1422) — the most learned Sufi of the medieval Deccan, central figure of Chishti Islam in southern India.
dedication: In memory of the Asaf Jahi dynasty
subtitle: Chishti · Gulbarga · 1321–1422

blocks.html:
The Saint of Gulbarga Lineage◆The Scholar◆Gulbarga
I · Lineage Delhi → Daulatabad · 1321

The most learned Sufi of the medieval Deccan

A Husayni Sayyid who took khilafat from Chiragh-i-Delhi, the last great Chishti of the north

Khwaja Sayyid Muhammad Husayni Gesudaraz Bandanawaz (b. Delhi 721 AH / 1321 CE; d. Gulbarga 825 AH / 1422 CE) is, by general agreement, the most learned and the most influential Sufi of the medieval Deccan. A Sayyid by descent (the family traced its lineage to Imam Husayn ibn 'Ali), born in Delhi and raised at Daulatabad after his family migrated south with Muhammad bin Tughluq's transfer, he returned to Delhi to study under Khwaja Nasir al-Din Chiragh-i-Delhi (d. 1356), the chief Chishti successor to Nizamuddin Awliya. He received khilafat from Chiragh-i-Delhi himself.

II · The Scholar Over a Hundred Works

A library in one lifetime

Quranic commentary, hadith and fiqh — and the earliest surviving prose in Dakhni

His scholarly output is the most extensive of any pre-modern Deccan Sufi. More than a hundred works are attributed to him, of which around thirty survive: commentaries on the Qur'an, treatises on hadith, manuals of Hanafi fiqh, expositions of the works of Ibn al-'Arabi, his own collected discourses (the Javami' al-Kalim, compiled by his disciple Sayyid Muhammad Akbar Husayni). He also wrote some of the earliest preserved prose in Dakhni Urdu — the Mi'raj al-'Ashiqin ("The Ascension of the Lovers"), a guide for spiritual seekers in the southern vernacular — making him a foundational figure in Dakhni literary history as well as in Sufism.

III · Gulbarga The Bahmani Invitation · 1399

The hospice at Gulbarga

An honoured but independent saint at the court of Firuz Shah Bahmani, and the shrine that outlived them both

At the invitation of the Bahmani sultan Firuz Shah Bahmani he moved to Gulbarga in 1399 and established his hospice there. His relationship with the sultan was respectful but independent: when Firuz Shah sought political endorsement, Bandanawaz declined and gave his blessing to the sultan's younger brother (the future Ahmad Shah I) instead. He died at Gulbarga in 1422 at the age of 101 (lunar) and was buried at his hospice. His dargah, built by Ahmad Shah I and successively enlarged, remains the largest Sufi shrine in the southern Deccan and the most-visited dargah in modern Karnataka. The annual 'urs, observed in the Islamic month of Dhu'l-Qa'da, draws hundreds of thousands of pilgrims.

blocks.eyebrow: A Chishti Life in the Deccan
blocks.title: Bandanawaz Gisudaraz in dates

items.text: Born in Delhi (721 AH) to a family of Husayni Sayyids; given the epithet Gesudaraz ("long-tressed") and later Bandanawaz ("cherisher of the poor").
Family migrates to Daulatabad following Muhammad bin Tughluq's forced transfer of Delhi's population to the new southern capital.
Returns to Delhi and enters the circle of Khwaja Nasir al-Din Chiragh-i-Delhi, the last great Chishti master of the north and chief successor to Nizamuddin Awliya.
Nasir al-Din Chiragh-i-Delhi dies; Bandanawaz carries his khilafat forward and becomes the principal Chishti spiritual heir in the Deccan.
Authors the Mi'raj al-'Ashiqin, a guide for spiritual seekers in Dakhni Urdu — among the earliest surviving prose compositions in the southern vernacular.
Scholarly corpus — Quranic commentaries, hadith treatises, Hanafi fiqh manuals, and expositions of Ibn al-'Arabi — grows to over a hundred attributed works; around thirty survive.
Accepts the invitation of Bahmani Sultan Firuz Shah Bahmani and moves to Gulbarga, establishing his khanqah there.
Declines to give political endorsement to Firuz Shah; instead bestows his blessing on the sultan's younger brother, the future Ahmad Shah I Bahmani.
Dies at Gulbarga (825 AH) at the age of 101 lunar years; interred at his hospice, which Ahmad Shah I converts into a dargah.
The dargah is enlarged by successive Bahmani and later rulers; it becomes the largest Sufi shrine in the southern Deccan.
The 'urs observed each year in Dhu'l-Qa'da draws hundreds of thousands of pilgrims; the dargah remains the most-visited Sufi shrine in modern Karnataka.
